subject: ap-csp
theme: Computing innovations are created through an iterative development process that requires collaboration, careful design, and systematic debugging.
core_concepts: A computing innovation uses a program as an essential component — it can be physical (a smart car), nonphysical software (a photo editing app), or a computing concept (machine learning). Identifying the type of innovation is a key CRD skill.
The development process is iterative — developers investigate, design, prototype, and test in repeated cycles. Feedback gathered after each prototype drives improvements before the next cycle begins.
Effective programming depends on defining clear requirements before writing code, then documenting behavior with comments so other developers can maintain the code without asking the original author.

Event-driven programming executes code in response to user actions or system signals (clicks, key presses, timer fires). Sequential programming runs statements top-to-bottom in a fixed order regardless of user interaction.

Programs can contain three types of errors: syntax errors (violation of language rules — code won't run), logic errors (incorrect results but program runs), and run-time errors (crashes during execution when given unexpected input).

Debugging is the systematic process of finding and fixing errors by comparing expected behavior to actual behavior — it often involves adding display statements or tracing variable values step by step.
Collaboration across diverse teams improves computing innovations by surfacing blind spots, reducing bias in design, and ensuring accessibility for a broader range of users.
exam_tip: The AP exam frequently asks you to distinguish event-driven from sequential programming and to classify error types — know that syntax errors prevent execution, logic errors allow execution but produce wrong results, and run-time errors crash during execution.
